summary: Gruber reviews the 2020 iPad Air, finding it nearly identical in design to the 11-inch iPad Pro but with key differences that matter. The Air shares the Pro's flat-sided industrial design and fits the same Magic Keyboard, but lacks ProMotion (120Hz) and Face ID. Surprisingly, the A14-equipped Air outperforms the A12Z-based iPad Pro in single-core and GPU benchmarks. The biggest practical difference is Touch ID vs Face ID, which becomes especially annoying when using a keyboard. Gruber finds the $600 base model compelling for handheld use but questions the value of the $750 256GB model when the Pro starts at $800.
key_points: The iPad Air is physically identical to the 11-inch iPad Pro in dimensions, differing only by 0.2mm in thickness and slightly wider bezels
The A14-powered iPad Air actually outperforms the A12Z iPad Pro in single-core CPU and GPU benchmarks, creating an odd product hierarchy
ProMotion (120Hz) remains a meaningful differentiator — noticeable in scrolling smoothness and Apple Pencil latency
Touch ID on the power button works fine for handheld use but is significantly worse than Face ID when using a keyboard, requiring a reach to the power button every time
The $750 256GB Air sits awkwardly close to the $800 128GB Pro, making its value proposition unclear
money_quotes: Apple provided me the green one — it is charmingly minty.
In addition to professional and premium, 'Pro', in Apple's parlance, also simply means 'more expensive'.

While writing this very section, specifically about the fact that I can't stop expecting Face ID to unlock the iPad Air while it's connected to the Magic Keyboard, I sat here stabbing at the space bar on the keyboard wondering why it's not unlocking.
Face ID is what puts much of the magic in the Magic Keyboard experience.
Conceptually it seems a bit mushy to have both Touch ID and Face ID on the same device.
If we, as humans, can recognize people we know while they're wearing face masks, computers can do it too.
I don't know who they are, if they're out there.
key_insight: The 2020 iPad Air is a compelling value at $600 for handheld use, but the lack of Face ID becomes a significant friction point with a keyboard, and its higher storage tiers sit uncomfortably close to Pro pricing.
